category_title: Students who use ostentation and masking to balance work and play
minimal_context: The student is waiting in line to practice straight jumps over a horse during a PE workshop.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Alright, it's time for the 'Flying' workshop. I want everyone doing straight jumps over the horse. Please line up.

STUDENT: (Whispering to a friend) There I make the jump, it's easy, I'm going to have fun. (Stands in line for the run-up)

TUTOR: Next person, go ahead.

STUDENT: (Executes a quick somersault instead of a straight jump, lands, and immediately returns to the back of the line to wait, pretending to fulfill expectations)

TUTOR: Keep practicing your jumps. I need to go take care of the other group for a minute.

STUDENT: (Sees the teacher leaving) The teacher can't see us, he's looking in the other direction. (Insults Aris who is preparing to jump. Laughs loudly when Aris misses and falls. Tries to flee as Aris pursues him, receives a violent blow on the shoulder) Stop, stop! (Laughs)

options.A: Teach the student a specific replacement behavior to use when waiting in line for the horse jump during the workshop.
options.B: Position yourself to discreetly monitor the students' behaviors, glancing to check they are working and listening for any noise unrelated to the PE lesson.
options.C: Respond with enthusiastic praise for the student's jump, expressing pride in their quick completion and encouraging them to keep going.
options.D: Alter the original lesson plans for the workshop and incorporate the student's spontaneous movements into the jumping activity.
